title: Lyapunov Exponents and Mixing in DiPerna-Lions Flows
speaker: Elia Bruè
abstract:
In 2003, Bressan proposed a conjecture on the mixing efficiency of incompressible flows, which remains open. This talk surveys progress toward resolving Bressan’s mixing conjecture and presents a new result confirming its asymptotic validity for time-periodic velocity fields. 
We accomplish this by adapting dynamical systems tools to the non-smooth framework of DiPerna-Lions flows. Furthermore, we discuss links to bounds on metric entropy and extensions of the Ruelle inequality.

proof_stages.label: Bressan mixing conjecture and geometric mixing scale
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Bressan’s conjecture for smooth incompressible flows on the torus, defines geometric mixing of a set or density down to scale epsilon, and interprets the conjecture as a universal logarithmic lower bound on the cost of rearrangement.

proof_stages.label: Functional mixing reformulation by negative Sobolev norms
proof_stages.summary: The geometric-scale formulation is recast in the more standard language of decay of correlations, where the H^{-1} norm of the transported scalar serves as a functional mixing scale expected to satisfy the same exponential lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Known p>1 theory and maximal-function barrier
proof_stages.summary: Existing positive results are reviewed: the conjectural bound is known when the cost is measured in L^p with p>1, because harmonic-analysis tools such as Hardy–Littlewood maximal estimates yield weak propagation of regularity but fail at the endpoint p=1.

proof_stages.label: Periodic-flow Lyapunov-exponent mixing bound
proof_stages.summary: For time-periodic smooth velocity fields, the main result bounds the asymptotic exponential rate of functional or geometric mixing by the spatial average of the top Lyapunov exponent, refining the principle that mixing cannot outpace gradient growth.

proof_stages.label: Comparison with Bressan cost and asymptotic conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The average top Lyapunov exponent is bounded above by the time-averaged L^1 norm of Du, so the Lyapunov mixing estimate implies an asymptotic version of Bressan’s conjecture and suggests any counterexample must mix anomalously fast only at short times.

proof_stages.label: DiPerna–Lions regular Lagrangian-flow framework
proof_stages.summary: The conjecture is placed in the natural nonsmooth setting of Sobolev velocity fields, where regular Lagrangian flows are selected by the measure-preservation or bounded-compression property and are equivalent to well-posed bounded solutions of the transport equation.

proof_stages.label: Weak differentials, Oseledets cocycles, and nonsmooth extension
proof_stages.summary: Quantitative DiPerna–Lions estimates give approximate differentiability for p>1, while a still weaker differential-in-measure forms a cocycle to which Oseledets’ theorem applies, allowing the smooth Lyapunov mixing theorem to extend to W^{1,p} regular Lagrangian flows for p>1.

proof_stages.label: Ruelle inequality and the open p=1 endpoint
proof_stages.summary: At the endpoint p=1 the weak differential is too poor to control mixing or entropy, but for p>1 the available approximate differentiability is strong enough to prove a nonsmooth Ruelle inequality bounding metric entropy by positive Lyapunov exponents; finiteness of entropy at p=1 remains open.

examples.name: Bressan’s one-dimensional rearrangement lemma
examples.note: The solved one-dimensional rearrangement-cost problem is presented as the heuristic source for expecting a logarithmic lower bound in the higher-dimensional mixing conjecture.

examples.name: Epsilon chessboard pattern
examples.note: A chessboard with tile size about epsilon is used only to visualize why mixing at scale epsilon automatically gives mixing at larger scales.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the epsilon chessboard pattern to reason through the mixing-scale definition, explaining how larger balls still see comparable black and white portions

examples.name: Alternating shear-flow toy model
examples.note: The restricted class of alternating shear flows is cited as a simplified setting where sharp exponential mixing bounds at the borderline have been proved, illustrating how hard the full problem is.
